title: Korhonen–Lokshtanov Question on Induced Disjoint Paths in Induced-Minor-Free Classes
statement_text: Is Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths NP-hard in $H$-induced-minor-free graphs for some fixed $k$ and $H$?
context_text: Korhonen and Lokshtanov posed this question to understand the tractability frontier of Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths when graphs are restricted by excluding a fixed induced minor. The problem is NP-complete in general graphs for $k=2$ but polynomial in planar and bounded-genus graphs, leaving the induced-minor-free setting open.
paper_contribution: Resolves the question affirmatively for $k=2$ and $H$ equal to the 1-subdivision of $K_{5}$ (or $K_{3,3}$): Induced 2-Disjoint Paths (and the stronger Induced Disjoint $S$–$T$ Paths with $|S|=|T|=2$) is NP-complete in string graphs, which exclude every 1-subdivision of a non-planar graph as an induced minor.
paper_title: Induced Disjoint Paths Without an Induced Minor

title: Chudnovsky–Seymour–Trotignon Question on Subcubic Induced Subdivision Containment
statement_text: Is there a subcubic graph $H$ such that deciding whether an input graph contains $H$ as an induced subdivision ($H$-\textsc{ISC}) is NP-complete?
context_text: Until this paper, every graph $H$ for which $H$-ISC was known to be NP-complete had a vertex of degree at least 4. Chudnovsky, Seymour, and Trotignon (the fourth author of the present paper) asked whether NP-hardness of induced subdivision containment can occur for subcubic graphs.
paper_contribution: Answers the question affirmatively: by leveraging the NP-completeness of Induced 2-Disjoint Paths in string graphs (Theorem 1.1), the paper exhibits a fixed subcubic graph $H$ for which $H$-ISC is NP-complete (Theorems 1.3–1.4).
notes: The abstract is truncated before the full attribution text; attributed to Chudnovsky, Seymour, and Trotignon based on context and the related bibliography entry [5] (Detecting an induced net subdivision, 2013); attributed_year left null as the precise source paper is uncertain.
paper_title: Induced Disjoint Paths Without an Induced Minor

title: Conjecture 1.5
statement_text: For any subcubic graph $H$, $H$-\textsc{ISC} is in $\mathsf{P}$ if and only if $H$ is planar.
context_text: The conjecture is proposed by the paper's authors as a natural open question following their proof that a specific non-planar subcubic graph yields NP-complete induced subdivision containment. It complements known polynomial-time algorithms for planar induced subdivision detection (e.g., for the net and $K_{4}$) and the new hardness results of the paper.
notes: The 'only if' direction (NP-hardness for non-planar subcubic $H$) implicitly assumes $\mathsf{P}\neq\mathsf{NP}$.
paper_title: Induced Disjoint Paths Without an Induced Minor

title: Informal Question on Complexity Gap Between Induced Disjoint Paths Variants
statement_text: Is there a hereditary graph class in which Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths is NP-complete but Induced Disjoint $S$–$T$ Paths with $|S|=|T|=k$ is polynomial-time solvable? The case $k=2$ is of particular interest.
context_text: The authors observe a structural asymmetry: polynomial-time algorithms in the literature tend to apply more broadly to Induced $k$-Disjoint Paths (the linkage variant), while hardness proofs tend to apply more broadly to Induced Disjoint $S$–$T$ Paths (the flow variant with $|S|=|T|=k$). This raises the question of whether the two problems can be separated in complexity on some hereditary class.
notes: Appears in running prose with 'we wonder if' in the preamble to the open questions section (context before Conjecture 1.5); no labelled theorem environment.
paper_title: Induced Disjoint Paths Without an Induced Minor
